title: Pork vindaloo
subtitle: Goan pork curry in a vinegar and chilli paste
excerpt: Pork shoulder marinated and braised in a wet paste of dried chillies, vinegar, garlic, and warm spices. The Goan curry that crossed into Anglo-Indian cooking and became Britain's benchmark for heat — properly made, it is sour and deeply spiced rather than merely hot.
sourceNotes: Based on the Goan vindaloo tradition documented in Floyd Cardoz, 'Floyd Cardoz: Flavorwalla' (2016) and Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005). Vindaloo descends from the Portuguese carne de vinha d'alhos (meat in wine and garlic), transformed by Goan cooks who substituted palm vinegar for wine and added the chillies that arrived with the Portuguese in the sixteenth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well. Cool completely and freeze in the sauce.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well; the flavour only improves.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Better the next day. Refrigerate after cooking and reheat gently.

content.text: A properly made vindaloo is not a competition in heat. The central flavour is vinegar, tart and mouth-puckering, balanced against warm spices: cumin, cinnamon, cardamom, cloves. The dried chillies provide intensity and colour, but the dish should be sour first. This is the tradition of Goa, where palm vinegar was the souring agent before Portuguese contact and the carne de vinha d'alhos evolved into something entirely new.

The paste is made by grinding dried chillies, garlic, and spices with red wine vinegar (a practical substitute for the original palm vinegar) into a thick, brick-red paste. The pork marinates in this for at least two hours, then braises covered with a small amount of water until the meat is tender and the sauce has reduced to coat it.

Toast {{cumin-seeds}} of cumin seeds, {{cinnamon-stick}} broken cinnamon stick, the seeds from {{cardamom-pods}} cardamom pods, {{cloves}} cloves, and {{black-pepper}} whole peppercorns in a dry pan for 2 minutes. Grind to a powder. Blend the ground spices with {{garlic}} garlic cloves, {{ginger-fresh}} ginger, {{chilli-flakes}} of chilli, {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric, and {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar to a smooth paste. Toss {{pork-shoulder}} of pork shoulder cubes in the paste with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Cover and refrigerate for at least 2 hours.

Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a Dutch oven over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} sliced onions and cook for 10 minutes until golden. Add the marinated pork and all the paste. Stir and cook for 5 minutes until the pork is lightly browned. Add 200 ml of water, bring to the boil, cover tightly, and reduce to a low simmer. Cook for 70–80 minutes until the pork is completely tender and the sauce is thick. Check and stir every 20 minutes, adding a small splash of water if the sauce catches. Rest for 10 minutes before serving.

items.symptom: Vindaloo is too hot to eat
items.cause: Too many chillies or very potent dried chillies
items.fix: Start with 1 teaspoon of chilli flakes and taste the paste before adding the pork. Serve with plain yoghurt alongside to cut the heat.

items.symptom: Sauce is acidic to the point of sharpness
items.cause: Too much vinegar, or not enough cooking time to mellow
items.fix: A long braise naturally softens the vinegar's edge. If still sharp after 70 minutes, add a small pinch of sugar and cook for another 10 minutes.

items.symptom: Pork is still chewy at 80 minutes
items.cause: Heat too low, or pork cut too large
items.fix: Shoulder needs a proper simmer to break down. Increase the heat slightly, add a splash of water, and continue for another 20 minutes.

content.text: Vindaloo is one of the clearest examples of colonial food exchange: a Portuguese preservation method (meat marinated in wine and garlic to extend its life at sea) adopted by Goan cooks, spiced with ingredients the Portuguese had themselves introduced to India, and returned to Europe as something entirely transformed. British troops stationed in Goa encountered it in the nineteenth century, and it travelled home with Anglo-Indian households and later with the first wave of Goan migrants to Britain. By the 1970s it had become a benchmark of British curry-house heat, a reputation that has overshadowed its original character as a sour and complex braise.
